explanation.text: Margaret arrives without an appointment after Moskowitz has apparently left and realizes that an earlier train or telephone call could have prevented the problem. Her regret supports the theme that planning ahead is important.

Margaret's central uncertainty is whether she should choose safe museum work or risk pursuing architectural photography. Her question to the expert expresses the central idea because she needs an honest judgment of her ability before choosing her future.

After seeing one photograph, Moskowitz cancels the waiting elevator, asks Margaret to follow him, and summons other architects to the conference room. Those commands propel the story because they show that her picture has impressed him enough to stop and evaluate her work.

Until paragraph 19, Moskowitz is determined to catch his train and refuses to look at the portfolio. Telling the elevator operator they no longer need him reverses that direction because Moskowitz now chooses to stay and examine the photographs.

Praise from alumni encourages Margaret, but she still doubts whether it reflects genuine professional ability. The architects' assurance most affects her because their expert judgment resolves the doubt about choosing photography.

The switchboard operator first tells Margaret she is out of luck but then helps identify Moskowitz; he initially refuses her and then stops after seeing one image. These uncertain reversals create suspense about whether Margaret will receive the expert judgment she needs.

Margaret pursues expert advice about photography but also applies to the museum as a safer employment option. Keeping that second career possibility means she does not depend on only one route to success.

Paragraph 2 describes Miyax's trembling hands, quickened heartbeat, desperate lack of food, and uncertainty that the wolves will help her. The tone becomes anxious because this urgent language leaves her survival in doubt.

Miyax knows that animals use signals, has successfully imitated squirrels, and studies the black wolf's role as the pack's wise leader. These details reveal a developed understanding of nature and animal behavior.

Although Miyax urgently needs food, she remains still rather than frightening the wolf because her father taught her patience with nature. This detail supports a theme about using learned patience and respect when facing a difficult problem.
Miyax moves slowly so she will not alarm Amaroq and hopes he will recognize the kindness in her eyes. Her careful behavior and wish for trust reveal a friendly, respectful attitude toward him.

Miyax remembers the terrifying realization that she is lost in an endless "ocean of grass," but her heart then leaps when Amaroq finally looks at her. The language combines danger and hope because fear returns just before the wolf's attention gives her a possible chance.

Aleks's escape requires destroying his record, reaching the stables unnoticed, getting Quicksilver, and finding an unknown way out before dinner ends. The many risky steps create suspense because readers cannot know which part may fail.

Aleks chooses the dinner shift because it leaves the barracks with only a minimal guard, and he knows he must leave before dinner ends. These details develop the idea that his plan depends on predictable army routines.

Shulga's office door and filing drawers are unlocked because he is too cocky to imagine anyone searching them. His overconfidence creates the careless security weakness that allows Aleks to enter and investigate.
Aleks begins the paragraph growling in frustration as repeated searches fail. His attitude turns to surprise when a tilted drawer reveals an unexpected false bottom.
Even while injured and uncertain of the route, Aleks insists that he will find a way out because one must exist. That determination best reflects pursuing a goal despite obstacles.

Aleks asks Quicksilver to remain quiet and not become excited so that no one discovers their departure. His caution reveals that he understands the escape is still in progress and further dangers may await them.

The Anglyan journal is concealed beneath a false drawer bottom in the office of an enemy officer, and Aleks risks taking it with him. This unusual secret discovery is important to a summary because it introduces a major new mystery, unlike incidental office or travel details.

The author first places readers in the imagined role of an ocean fish and playfully calls them "my finned fishy friends." The phrase builds connection because its direct, humorous address invites readers into the topic.

SoFi is artificial, yet real fish do not flee from her and sometimes swim beside her. Their response best supports the idea that she behaves enough like a fish to avoid seeming like a typical intrusive robot.

Calling robotic fish "essential" for understanding and protecting threatened marine life is an evaluation of their value. This language most clearly reveals the author's positive view of SoFi's potential.

Paragraph 8 calls SoFi an engineering challenge, and paragraphs 12 through 14 list the requirements engineers had to solve: waterproofing, buoyancy, weight, communication, and compact equipment. Those details show them focusing on design needs to achieve the robot-fish goal.

Unlike loud, tethered marine vehicles that frighten animals, SoFi swims without a boat cable or noisy propeller and does not disturb nearby fish. This contrasts with environmental harm because the technology observes wildlife without disrupting it.

A shark attacking SoFi would produce rare footage of a real animal interacting naturally with the robot. Katzschmann welcomes that possibility because observing animal interactions is one of the project's research goals.

The article emphasizes that SoFi can observe animals without frightening them and may help biologists study behavior, ecosystems, and pollution. Her importance as a tool for understanding ocean life therefore belongs in a summary more than individual technical features do.
